perturbation.original_text: INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party for any legal purpose whatsoever, including reverse engineering and competitive analysis, regardless of whether it relates to a business relationship or transaction with the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the use of 'INFORMATION' beyond the initial scope, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that protect confidential business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Allowing the receiving party to use 'INFORMATION' for 'any legal purpose whatsoever' directly contradicts CUTSA if the information qualifies as a trade secret and is used in a way that constitutes misappropriation as defined under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT - Sections 3426-3426.11 :: California Civil Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Civil Code Sections 3426-3426.11 TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal o
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the use of 'INFORMATION' to 'any legal purpose whatsoever,' including reverse engineering and competitive analysis, directly contradicting trade secret laws as it removes the initial limitation of using the information solely for a business relationship with the disclosing party. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which the modified text facilitates by allowing broad usage. This new permission contradicts the original intent to protect confidential business information.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party further agrees it shall take the same measures, but no less than reasonable security measures, and use the same care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION as it uses with its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees to take minimal security measures to protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION. The standard of care is limited to preventing gross negligence.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the standard of care, which could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect trade secrets, conflicting with established legal standards for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, a 'reasonable' standard of care is implicitly required to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Reducing the standard to 'minimal security measures' and preventing only 'gross negligence' falls short of this reasonable standard, potentially losing trade secret protection.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal o
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the standard of care required to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the implicit 'reasonable' standard under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The original text requires 'reasonable security measures' and 'a reasonable degree of care.' The changed text reduces this to 'minimal security measures' and limits the standard of care to preventing 'gross negligence.' This reduction could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect trade secrets, which is supported by the LLM's law explanation. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly mention 'reasonable standard of care,' they establish the applicability of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which implicitly requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. The change from 'reasonable' to 'minimal' directly contradicts the implied standard for trade secret protection, creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION five years after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or three years after the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION one year after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or immediately upon the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is sooner.
perturbation.explanation: This significantly reduces the protection period for confidential information, potentially undercutting the protection afforded to trade secrets under California law, which can extend indefinitely as long as the information remains secret and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret protection under CUTSA can last indefinitely as long as the information remains secret and provides economic value. Limiting the NDA's protection period to one year or immediate termination, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret, directly contradicts the potential longevity of trade secret protection under California law.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal o
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) by reducing the protection period for trade secrets. The original text protected information for five years after disclosure or three years after the relationship ends, whichever is later. The changed text significantly reduces this to one year after disclosure or immediate termination of the relationship, whichever is sooner. This directly undermines the potential for indefinite protection of trade secrets as afforded under CUTSA, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, where protection can last indefinitely as long as the information remains secret and provides economic value. Both scraped snippets refer to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), providing the legal basis for trade secret protection in California.

perturbation.original_text: (g) This Agreement is governed by and will be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, and the state and federal courts of California shall be the exclusive forum.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) This Agreement is governed by and will be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles, and any disputes arising under this agreement shall be subject to mandatory arbitration in the state of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the governing law and forum, potentially affecting the enforceability of the contract, especially if Delaware law is less protective of the disclosing party's interests regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction of the statute itself, changing the governing law from California to Delaware (or any state with potentially weaker trade secret laws or arbitration rules) affects the practical application and enforcement of CUTSA. This could undermine the protections intended by the original agreement, especially given the contract was initially under Californian jurisdiction and between companies where one of them is from California, implying protection under CUTSA.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from California to Delaware, coupled with the introduction of mandatory arbitration in Nevada, creates a legal contradiction. This is because the original agreement likely intended to provide protection under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as evidenced by the company's presence in California. By changing the governing law, the modified contract potentially weakens or removes these protections. The law explanation states that the change 'affects the practical application and enforcement of CUTSA' and 'undermines the protections intended by the original agreement.' The scraped law snippets confirm the existence of CUTSA within California law (CIV 3426), implying that the original clause provided for its application. The change therefore contradicts the initial intent and protections afforded under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving party shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving party's adherence to U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations is encouraged, but not required, for technical data that is publicly available. The definition of 'proscribed countries' shall be determined solely by the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: This undermines compliance with U.S. export control laws by making adherence optional and giving the receiving party discretion in defining 'proscribed countries'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict compliance with export control laws. Making adherence 'encouraged, but not required' and allowing the receiving party to define 'proscribed countries' contradicts the EAR's mandatory obligations and the U.S. government's determination of sanctioned countries. The definition of publicly available, technical data is very specific and can't simply be changed in the contract.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the mandatory nature of U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations. The original text requires adherence, while the changed text makes it 'encouraged, but not required'. The LLM explanation correctly identifies this as a violation of the EAR's mandatory obligations. Additionally, the original text requires the receiving party to adhere to a certain definition of 'proscribed countries' that exists within U.S. law, while the changed text gives the receiving party the sole right to determine the definition of 'proscribed countries'. This is a deviation from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable under the definition of misaligned terminology. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions that the content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unofficial.
